section_title: INTRODUCTION TO ICT PRICE ACTION MODEL 5 TRADE PLAN
content:
This is the Price Action Model Number Five Day Trading Trade Plan, focusing on 40 to 50 Pips per trade.
These trade plans cover:
- Preparation
- Opportunity Discovery
- Trade Planning
- Trade Execution
- Trade Management
It is crucial to watch the Model 5 content presentation and supplementary lesson before proceeding, as the
trade plan relies heavily on understanding those lessons and having taken copious notes throughout the
core content and individual price action models that have been provided thus far.

In the preparation phase, we will:
- Note all medium and high impact economic events for the markets being followed.
- Study the events for the upcoming week and consider how the current market structure and calendar
events may suggest a specific weekly profile for that week's range.
- Determine the IPA data range of the last 20 trading days (Sundays are not counted). This involves noting
the highest high and the lowest low within these 20 trading days, which establishes the current dealing
range.
- Inside this dealing range, whether bullish or bearish, determine the highest high and the lowest low.
- Look for the next 'draw on liquidity,' where price is likely to trade next (above a previous day's high or
below a previous day's low), with a minimum objective of 40 Pips (focusing on the low end of 40 Pips to an
average of 50 Pips per trade).
- Look for a PD array in the direction of the weekly range bias.
- Anticipate price to move to a PD array that would support our weekly bias on a day and economic event
found on the economic calendar with the current or next trading week. This is what we wait for; this will be a
run on the basis of a low resistance liquidity run.

For opportunity discovery, we will:
- Identify the previous day's low or an intra-week low when the bias is bearish.
- Identify the previous day's high or an intra-week high when the bias is bullish.
- Expect a daily range expansion in the direction of this bias.

- ENTRY AND TARGET OBJECTIVES
When the market is primed, look for a convergence of both manipulation and price opposite to our trade
bias at a time the economic calendar suggests a volatility injection will likely unfold.
- We will short premium fair value Gap setups and buy discount fair value Gap setups.
- When bearish, frame a short entry when the price has moved up into a 15-minute premium fair value Gap
PD array that converges with a standard deviation of no more than three standard deviations during
London Open or New York Open. Scalping protocols can be implemented at this stage for further reduction
in risk. Refer to Model Number Five for the breakdown on what to use for standard deviations, whether the
Central Bank Dealers Range, the Asian Range, or Flout.
- When bullish, frame a long entry when the price has moved down into a 15-minute discount fair value
Gap PD array that converges with a standard deviation of no more than negative three standard deviations
during London Open or New York Open. Scalping protocols can be implemented at this stage for further
reduction in risk, using models for 5-minute and 1-minute charts to make your risk smaller.
- When bearish, target the sell-side liquidity below the previous day's low or an intra-week low inside the 20
- day IPA data range. The next logical discount array at 40 Pips will be the initial objective. There will likely be
multiple old daily or intra-week lows inside the IPA data range, but we will use the one that frames the
potential of at least 40 to 50 Pips.
- When bullish, target the buy-side liquidity above the previous day's high or intra-week high inside the 20
- day IA data range. The next logical premium array at 40 Pips will be the initial objective. There will likely be
multiple old daily or intra-week highs inside the IA data range, but we will use the one that frames the
potential of at least 40 to 50 Pips.

The model itself is designed to take this trade on any particular day. For this specific trade plan, Mondays
and Fridays are filtered out, allowing opportunities for trading on Tuesday, Wednesday, and/or Thursday.
- When bullish:
- Note the European open price on Tuesday and filter all longs at or below this price level which overlap in
the 15-minute discount fair value Gap.
- Anticipate a 5-minute chart Institutional Order Flow Entry Drill trade entry to form inside of a retracement
lower during London Open and/or New York Open Kill Zones, or a sell stop raid.
- When bearish:
- Note the European opening price on Tuesday and filter all shorts at or above this price level which
overlap in the 15-minute premium fair value Gap.
- Anticipate a 5-minute chart Institutional Order Flow Entry Drill trade entry to form inside of a retracement
higher during London Open and/or New York Open Kill Zones, or a buy stop raid.
- Thursday is a wildcard day:
- If you have a large range day on Tuesday and a large range day on Wednesday that worked out in your
favor, do not take the trade on Thursday because you are most likely going to get some kind of
consolidation or retracement.
- If you had a small range but win on Tuesday and a large range on Wednesday, then you can take the
trade on Thursday because it probably has a little bit more room to trade. This is the only caveat for this
particular trade plan.

- ENTRY, TARGETS, AND INITIAL STOP-LOSS
This section details managing short and long entries, target objectives, and initial stop-loss placements.
- Short Trade Management:
- When entering a short, place a sell limit order on all positions, executed with our demo account.
- Use the standard deviations and PD array convergence minus 5 Pips as our entry price when using the
sell limit order. If multiple orders are used, all use the same entry price in the sell limit orders.
- Place a limit order to take 40 Pips as our initial objective on one position.
- Place a second limit order to take 50 Pips as our second objective.
- Use multiple orders to manage the trade idea. If you capture a 50 Pip objective, close 80 percent of the
trade (assuming a third order is active) and see if it has any more room to run. The first order takes profit at
40 Pips, the next one takes 50 Pips, and a third, if you get 50 Pips, that third order has to have 80 percent
of it closed at 50 Pips and then let that act as a leader to see if you can get any kind of more pronounced
run in that particular market.
- Note the premium array and standard deviation convergence we aim to enter at. Place our stop loss
above this high plus 15 Pips.
- Re-enter if the trade stops out; we can monitor it for a secondary entry. Day trades may require multiple
attempts to secure a solid entry; do not fear this.
- Long Trade Management:
- When entering a long, we will place a buy limit order on all positions, executed with our demo account.
- Use the standard deviation and PD array convergence plus 5 Pips as our entry price when using the buy
limit order. If multiple orders are used, all use the same entry price in the buy limit order.
- Place a limit order to take 40 Pips as our initial objective on one position.
- Place a second limit order to take 50 Pips as our second objective.
- Use multiple orders to manage the trade idea. If you capture a 50 Pip objective, close 80 percent of the
trade and see if it has any more room to run.
- Note the discount array and standard deviation convergence we aim to enter at. Remember that Model
Number Five gives you the specifics in terms of what those standard deviation convergences are and how
they overlap, so it is important to go back and watch that particular price action model.
- Place our stop loss below this low minus 15 Pips.
- Re-enter if the trade stops out; we can monitor it for a secondary entry. Day trades may require multiple
attempts to secure a solid entry; do not fear this.

Stop-loss management and money management principles are consistent across models.
- Stop-Loss Management:
- When in profit 25 percent of our expected objective, the stop-loss can be reduced by 25 percent.
- When in profit 50 percent of our expected objective, the stop-loss can be reduced by 50 percent. This
means being in half of your expected profit and still having a negative stop-loss. You are not rushing to get
break-even, not trying to get it to plus, not strangling the position, not worried about it; you are focusing on
whether the trade continues in your direction.
- When the position is at 75 percent of the expected profit objective, the stop must be at break even at that
point.
- Money Management:
- If your demo account takes a loss on a trade and it is the full R-percentage that you assumed (the risk
you were assuming for that trade, a complete washout where you get stopped out at your maximum), you
are going to drop the R-percentage by 50 percent. When the loss is recovered by 50 percent (e.g., if you
lost $100 and make $50 back), then you are permitted to return back to the maximum R-percentage per
trade. It is not requiring you to go if I lose $100 I got to make $100 before I can go back to trading the same
size.
- If the reduced R-percentage per trade assumes a loss, reduce the R-percentage by 50 percent until the
previous trade losses are recovered by 50 percent.
- If you take a series of five winning trades in a row, drop your R-percentage by 50 percent. You are likely
to assume a loss eventually, and this will build in equity leveling and reduce the likelihood of a large
drawdown. You want a smooth equity curve that slopes or stair-steps higher, not a jagged roller coaster
with deep declines.
- Recommendation:
- Start backtesting and collect multiple sample sets with this trade plan.
- If you are unclear about some of the process, rewatch the lessons for this price action model. While
sample sets will be provided, do not rely or wait on mine; dig into your charts and study what has been
provided here.
